title: SEGMENT 11 — The first targeted drug: imatinib for CML

lines.text: SEGMENT 11 — The first targeted drug: imatinib for CML
I want to spend a whole segment on a single drug, because it changed the entire mental model of what cancer therapy could be.
The drug is imatinib, brand name Gleevec, and the disease is chronic myeloid leukemia, CML.
To appreciate the story, picture the world of cancer therapy in the 1990s.
The treatments we had were sledgehammers — broad chemotherapies that killed any rapidly dividing cell, cancer or not.
They were designed on the principle: cancer cells divide a lot, so let's poison anything that divides.
It worked, sometimes, by margins.
Your hair cells divide a lot, so you go bald.
Your gut lining divides a lot, so you vomit.
Your bone marrow divides a lot, so your blood counts crash.
Cancer divides a lot, so it gets hit too, slightly harder if you're lucky.
We called this carpet-bombing for good reason.
The drug carpet-bombs the cell-division process; you survive because the cancer happens to be a bit more vulnerable than you are.
Most patients with metastatic cancer died within months.
Then in 1960, two pathologists in Philadelphia named Nowell and Hungerford noticed something.
In nearly every patient with CML they examined, one of the chromosomes was visibly truncated under the microscope.
They called it the Philadelphia chromosome.
It was the first time a specific chromosomal abnormality had been linked to a specific cancer.
Nobody knew what it meant yet.
In 1973, Janet Rowley, a quietly persistent researcher at the University of Chicago, used improved chromosome-banding techniques to figure out exactly what had happened.
The Philadelphia chromosome wasn't simply a fragment — it was the product of a translocation, a swap of material between chromosome 9 and chromosome 22.
A piece of chromosome 9 had broken off and joined chromosome 22, and a piece of chromosome 22 had broken off and joined chromosome 9.
The exchange created a fusion of two genes that should never have met — a gene called BCR on chromosome 22 and a gene called ABL on chromosome 9.
The fusion gene encoded a fusion protein, BCR-ABL.
Through the 1980s and 90s, the molecular biology of BCR-ABL got worked out.
ABL by itself is a kinase — an enzyme that sticks phosphate groups onto other proteins to send growth signals — and it's normally carefully regulated.
When you stick BCR onto the front of ABL, you get a kinase that's permanently switched on.
The fusion protein continuously fires the growth signal in white blood cell precursors in the bone marrow, and the result, over time, is CML.
Now here's where the story gets interesting.
A scientist named Brian Druker, along with chemists at the company Ciba-Geigy (which later became Novartis), set out to do something that, at the time, sounded almost naive.
They asked: can we design a small molecule that fits into the ABL kinase pocket and shuts off the fusion protein, and only the fusion protein, and use that as a cancer drug?
Everyone else said this would never work as a strategy.
Kinases were too similar to each other; you'd hit dozens of off-targets.
The kinase wouldn't be a strong enough vulnerability.
CML would mutate around it.
And anyway, this was a niche disease.
They tried anyway.
The molecule that came out of the program was eventually called imatinib.
It fit beautifully into the ABL kinase pocket.
It didn't hit too many other kinases.
And in early trials, patients who had been on the slow march toward death from CML showed astonishing responses.
Their leukemia cell counts crashed within weeks.
Their bone marrows went back to looking normal.
The Philadelphia chromosome became undetectable in many of them.
The drug was approved by the FDA in 2001 after one of the fastest approval timelines in oncology history.
The ten-year survival rate for CML before imatinib was something like twenty percent.
After imatinib, it's something like ninety percent.
Patients on imatinib lead essentially normal lives, taking a pill a day, with their leukemia in deep suppression for decades.
CML went from being a disease that killed you in a few years to being a disease that you live with the way you'd live with high blood pressure.
The lesson the field absorbed from imatinib was: it is possible to design a key that only opens the cancer's specific lock.
You don't have to carpet-bomb.

If you understand exactly what's broken at the molecular level, and the broken thing is a kinase or other druggable protein, you can build a molecule that targets that one thing and leave the rest of the body alone.
Side effects of imatinib are mild compared to chemotherapy because the drug isn't poisoning everything that divides; it's poisoning one specific abnormal enzyme.
After imatinib, the floodgates opened.
The field went looking for more cancer driver mutations to drug.
EGFR in lung cancer.
HER2 in breast cancer.
BRAF in melanoma.
ALK in lung cancer.
KIT in gastrointestinal stromal tumors.
Each one is a story of finding the broken molecular machinery and building a drug that fits its specific pocket.
Targeted therapy, born from one fused-up chromosome in a leukemia, became the dominant paradigm.
We'll talk later about how cancers eventually mutate their way around these drugs — they do — but the imatinib moment was a genuine turning point.
It proved cancer could be more than a disease of organs; it could be a disease of specific, identifiable, druggable molecular wiring.
